taskVersion.name: epu_q2_medium
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_medium")
def epu_q2_medium(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, medium_pre, medium_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
To get a fix on Hicks, Muse, Tate & Furst Inc. in the aftermath of its fall from grace, ltok at the deals that aren't getting done.The Dallas investment firm came up short in its $5 billion bid for Italy's yellow pages a week ago. It hasn't found a buyer for Pinnacle Ftods Corp., which it put up for sale in March. It had to postpone plans for a public offering of Britain's yellow pages when financial markets turned sour over Enron Corp. and Iraq.And all of this is, well, just fine with founder and chairman Tom Hicks, who says he likes the way his company is emerging from the darkest days in its 15-year history.In a beaten-down market, Hicks Muse may not be landing as many deals as it once did, but it approaches its business with a hardheaded, disciplined attitude, intent on avoiding million-dollar mistakes."The firm has never been run in a better way than it's being run today in terms of day-to-day operations," Mr. Hicks said.A gtod message, though, will only go so far. In the hot-box of private equity deal-making, nothing matters in the end but results."The message has gotten out there, and everyone takes them at their word," said David Toll, managing editor at Private Equity Analyst, an industry publication."The real question is how they're going to do in the investments they've done in the past few years. I don't think anyone knows that."Hicks Muse controls $11 billion in assets. If it is indeed a better company, the reason is hard experience.After more than $1 billion in 

[...TRUNCATED...]

's yellow pages. Yell expanded eight months later by adding America's No. 2 yellow pages with a $600 million acquisition of McLeod isSA Publishing Co.A year ago, Hicks Muse saw an opportunity to double its money by selling half of its stake in Yell to investors.But "the market dropped. That wasn't possible," Mr. Hicks said. "We didn't need to go public. We had plenty of cash flow. The plan then was to wait until there was a different window."Is it now?Mr. Hicks says maybe yes, maybe no. What he wants is for Hicks Muse to get the decision right.E-mail ralm@dallasnews.comBACK TO INVESTMENT BASICSThree years ago, Hicks, Muse, Tate & Furst adopted a back-to-basics strategy in the wake of money-losing investments. Following are recent deals, some involving other investors:Company Date Business PriceSOisRCE: Dallas Morning News researchPinnacle Ftods Corp. May 2002 Beef, pork $1.4 billionPremier International Ftods May 2002 Nestle brands $176 millionAster City Cable November 2002 Poland cable TV $109 millionCaption:CHART(S): (Staff graphic) BACK TO INVESTMENT BASICS; PHOTO(S): (BOB MADER/Special Contributor) Name partners at Hicks, Muse, Tate & Furst include (from left) John Muse, Tom Hicks and Jack Furst. The Dallas-based investment firm is intent on avoiding million-dollar mistakes.
TARGET ARTICLE END
